C Porque present continuous só existe no passado.
2. Para um plano JÁ decidido (lançar a v3 em junho), qual forma é a mais natural?
A "is going to release" (plano decidido).
B "releases" (presente simples).
C "released" (passado).
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "We going to deliver it in June."
B "We are going to deliver it in June."
C "We will to deliver it in June."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Future Forms

GRAMMAR

Como falar de planos com três formas de futuro (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
will + base verbprevisão, promessa ou oferta. Depois de "will", verbo no infinitivo SEM "to".It will save time.
be going to + base verbplano ou intenção JÁ decidida. Sempre com am / is / are antes.We are going to release v3.
am / is / are + verb-ingarranjo fixo, com hora ou data marcada (present continuous).We are launching on Tuesday.
Negative / Question"I will not promise a date." / "Are you going to send it?"will not + verbo; Are you going to...?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"In June, we going to release version three."
"After 'will' comes the base verb: we will the app in May."
"The date is fixed, so we are the app on Tuesday."
"I think the new reports save you time."
"That feature is still on the for a future release."

Stage 2: Put the Roadmap Update in Order

Order

Coloque a atualização de roadmap numa ordem lógica.

?One request is still on the backlog, so I will be honest about the date.
?Thanks for joining. Let me walk you through what is coming.
?Next quarter, we are going to review the backlog with you.
?First, in June we are going to release version three of the dashboard.
?Then, we are launching the new mobile app on Tuesday the fifteenth.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

In June, we are going to release version three.
Em junho, vamos lançar a versão três.
We are launching the mobile app on Tuesday.
Vamos lançar o app no celular na terça-feira.
The new reports will save your team time.
Os novos relatórios vão economizar tempo da sua equipe.
That feature is still on the backlog.
Esse recurso ainda está no backlog (fila de espera).
We are going to review the requests together.
Vamos revisar os pedidos juntos.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A customer asks what is coming next quarter. You share a plan you have already decided. You say:
A "We are going to release version three."
B "We released version three."
C "We will to release version three."
You give a fixed launch date on the calendar. You say:
A "We will launching the app on Tuesday."
B "We are launching the app on Tuesday the fifteenth."
C "We launch maybe the app on Tuesday."
A customer asks for a feature you have not built yet. You are honest. You say:
A "We already delivered it last year."
B "It is on the backlog; we are still working on the estimate."
C "We will to build it right now."
You make a confident prediction about a benefit of the new app. You say:
A "The new app will save your agents time."
B "The new app saving time."
C "The new app going to saves time."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Think of your real product or project. Present a short roadmap: use "going to" for one decided release, the present continuous for one launch with a date, and "will" for one benefit. Be honest about one request that is still on the backlog. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1In June, we are going to release version three.Em junho, vamos lançar a versão três.
2We are launching the app on Tuesday.Vamos lançar o app na terça-feira.
3The new reports will save you time.Os novos relatórios vão economizar seu tempo.
4That request is on the backlog.Esse pedido está no backlog.
5We are going to review it together next quarter.Vamos revisar isso juntos no próximo trimestre.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Giving & Receiving Feedback

Giving honest, kind feedback and receiving it well. Key words: feedback, strength, improvement, acknowledge, appreciate, constructive, perspective, align, takeaway, tone. Structure: diplomatic softening (perhaps / it might be better to / I'd suggest / have you considered).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Feedback -- comments about your work, to help you improve (feedback, retorno)
"Thank you for the feedback on my demo."
Strength -- something a person does very well (ponto forte)
"Your clear demo was a real strength."
Improvement -- a change that makes something better (melhoria)
"There is one small area for improvement."
Acknowledge -- to accept that something is true or fair (reconhecer, admitir)
"I acknowledge that I rushed the slide."
Appreciate -- to be grateful for something (agradecer, valorizar)
"I appreciate your honest feedback."
Constructive -- helpful and meant to improve, not to criticize (construtivo)
"She always gives constructive feedback."
Perspective -- the way a person sees or thinks about something (perspectiva)
"May I share my perspective?"
Align -- to agree and move in the same direction (alinhar)
"Let's align on the next steps."
Takeaway -- the main point you remember and act on (conclusão principal)
"My takeaway is to send the deck earlier."
Tone -- the feeling or attitude in the way you speak (tom)
"Keep the tone warm and respectful."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Feedback
Strength
Improvement
Acknowledge
Appreciate
Constructive
Perspective
Align
Takeaway
Tone

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

In her one-on-one, David told Patrícia that her demo was a real strength. She appreciated the feedback and asked about any area for improvement. Instead of saying "you talked too fast," David kept the tone warm: "Perhaps we could slow down on the pricing slide, and it might be better to share the deck first." Patrícia acknowledged the point calmly. "I'd suggest we align on the agenda earlier," she added. "Have you considered sending it the day before?" Her takeaway was clear, and the feedback stayed constructive from start to finish.

1. Por que David diz "Perhaps we could slow down" em vez de "You talk too fast"?
A Porque ele não tem certeza do que aconteceu.
B Porque suaviza a crítica e mantém o tom diplomático.
C Porque é uma ordem direta e firme.
2. "Have you considered sending it the day before?" usa esta estrutura porque:
A Oferece uma ideia como pergunta, de forma gentil (consider + verbo-ing).
B É uma acusação sobre um erro do passado.
C Fala de uma rotina que se repete toda semana.
3. Which sentence gives feedback most diplomatically?
A "You should have tested it."
B "Next time, it might be better to test it first."
C "Your work is wrong."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Diplomatic Softening

GRAMMAR

Como suavizar feedback e sugestões para soar diplomático (explicação em inglês e português).

SoftenerUse / UsoExample
Perhaps we could...Suaviza uma sugestão. Softens a suggestion.Perhaps we could check this.
It might be better to + verboSugere uma mudança gentilmente. Suggests a change gently.It might be better to slow down.
I'd suggest (that)...Dá conselho com educação (sem "to": suggest + frase). Polite advice.I'd suggest we align first.
Have you considered + verbo-ing?Oferece a ideia como pergunta. Offers an idea as a question.Have you considered sending it earlier?
Avoid / EviteNão use o acusatório "you should have..." → troque por "next time we could..."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

" we could check this part again."
"It might be to send the deck first."
"I'd we align on the agenda."
"Have you testing it first?"
"I really your honest feedback."
"My is to send the deck earlier."

Stage 2: Give Feedback in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de dar feedback construtivo num 1:1 na ordem correta.

?Agree a clear takeaway and align on next steps.
?Lead with a genuine strength.
?Ask for their perspective in return.
?Soften one area for improvement (perhaps / it might be better to).
?Offer an idea as a question (have you considered...?).

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

Perhaps we could check this part again.
Talvez pudéssemos revisar esta parte de novo.
It might be better to slow down a little.
Talvez seja melhor ir um pouco mais devagar.
Thank you, I really appreciate the feedback.
Obrigada, eu realmente agradeço o feedback.
Have you considered sharing the deck earlier?
Você já pensou em compartilhar o material antes?
My takeaway is to align on the agenda.
Minha conclusão é alinhar a pauta.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A teammate rushed the pricing slide. You want to say it kindly. You say:
A "You went too fast and it was bad."
B "It might be better to slow down on the pricing slide."
C "You should have gone slower."
Your manager gives you feedback. The best way to receive it is:
A "Thank you, I appreciate that. My takeaway is to send the deck earlier."
B "That is not my fault."
C "Why are you always criticizing me?"
You want to offer an idea gently, as a question. Which is correct?
A "Have you considered to send it earlier?"
B "Have you considered sending it earlier?"
C "You must send it earlier."
You disagree, but want to stay diplomatic. You say:
A "I see it a little differently. May I share my perspective?"
B "You are completely wrong."
C "No. Do it again."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you give a teammate feedback after a call. Lead with a strength, then soften one area for improvement (perhaps / it might be better to / have you considered). End with a clear takeaway. Keep the tone warm. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1Perhaps we could check this part again.Talvez pudéssemos revisar esta parte de novo.
2It might be better to slow down a little.Talvez seja melhor ir um pouco mais devagar.
3Thank you, I really appreciate the feedback.Obrigada, eu realmente agradeço o feedback.
4Have you considered sharing the deck earlier?Você já pensou em compartilhar o material antes?
5My takeaway is to align on the agenda.Minha conclusão é alinhar a pauta.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Follow-ups & Action Items

Turning a meeting into clear tasks and reporting what was agreed. Key words: action item, owner, next step, assign, due, minutes, task, outstanding, circle back, wrap up. Structure: reported speech (said / told + backshift).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Action item -- a specific task that someone must do after a meeting (item de ação / tarefa definida)
"We turned the discussion into clear action items."
Owner -- the person who is responsible for one task (responsável pela tarefa)
"Each action item has an owner and a due date."
Next step -- the following action you take to move the work forward (próximo passo)
"We agreed on the next step before we closed the call."
Assign -- to give a task to a specific person (atribuir / designar)
"We assigned the follow-up email to the support team."
Due -- the day by which a task must be finished (com prazo / a vencer)
"The onboarding deck is due on Friday."
Minutes -- the written notes that record what a meeting decided (ata da reunião)
"Patrícia will share the minutes with the client today."
Task -- a piece of work that one person has to do (tarefa)
"The onboarding deck is the main task right now."
Outstanding -- still not done or finished; still open (pendente / em aberto)
"The billing question is still outstanding."
Circle back -- to return to a topic or person at a later time (voltar a tratar depois)
"Let's circle back to the billing question on Monday."
Wrap up -- to finish or bring a meeting or task to an end (encerrar / finalizar)
"Before we wrapped up, we reviewed the open items."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Action item
Owner
Next step
Assign
Due
Minutes
Outstanding
Circle back
Wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

After the sync, Patrícia wrote the minutes and reported the team's decisions. Priya said the dashboard was ready for the client. David told the team he would send the technical notes by Wednesday. Priya also said she had finished the first report. Everyone told Patrícia they would confirm their tasks by the end of the day.

1. Por que usamos "told the team" e não "said the team"?
A Porque "told" só existe no passado.
B Porque "tell" precisa de uma pessoa depois (told the team / told me); "say" não.
C Porque "told" significa "perguntou".
2. A fala original foi "The dashboard is ready". Por que reportamos como "was ready"?
A Porque na fala reportada recuamos o verbo no tempo: is → was.
B Porque "was" e "is" significam a mesma coisa.
C Porque o dashboard quebrou depois.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "He said me he would send the notes."
B "He told me he would send the notes."
C "He told that he would send the notes."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Como reportar o que alguém disse (explicação em inglês e português).

Point / PontoRule / RegraExample
sayNão leva pessoa depois. "say (that)..."She said (that) it was ready.
tellSEMPRE leva pessoa: "tell me / the team".She told me (that) it was ready.
backshiftRecue o verbo: is→was, have→had, will→would, can→could."I will send it" → He said he would send it.
erro comumNunca "said me"; nunca "told that" sem pessoa.✓ He told me... / ✓ He said that...

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"Priya us the dashboard was ready."
"David that the data was ready."
"He said he send the notes by Wednesday."
"She said she finished the report."
"The billing question is still ."

Stage 2: Put the Recap in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um recap de reunião na ordem correta.

?Note what is still outstanding and circle back to it later.
?Open the minutes and report what the team said.
?Wrap up and share the minutes with the client.
?Turn the discussion into clear action items.
?Assign an owner and a due date to each item.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

Priya said the dashboard was ready.
A Priya disse que o painel estava pronto.
David told the team he would send the notes.
O David disse ao time que enviaria as notas.
Each action item has an owner and a due date.
Cada item de ação tem um responsável e um prazo.
The billing question is still outstanding.
A questão do faturamento ainda está pendente.
Let's circle back to it on Monday.
Vamos voltar a tratar disso na segunda-feira.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

You report what a colleague told you personally. You say:
A "He said me he would send the notes."
B "He told me he would send the notes."
C "He told he would send the notes."
In the meeting Priya said "The data is ready." Later you report:
A "Priya said the data is ready."
B "Priya said the data was ready."
C "Priya told the data was ready."
A task is not finished yet. You describe its status:
A "It is the owner."
B "It is still outstanding."
C "It is the minutes."
You want to return to a topic later in the week. You say:
A "Let's circle back to it on Monday."
B "Let's assign it on Monday."
C "Let's wrap up to it on Monday."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Think of a real meeting you ran this week. Recap it: report what your team said with "said" and "told", name the owner of each action item, and say what is still outstanding. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1Priya said the dashboard was ready.A Priya disse que o painel estava pronto.
2David told the team he would send the notes.O David disse ao time que enviaria as notas.
3Each action item has an owner and a due date.Cada item de ação tem um responsável e um prazo.
4The billing question is still outstanding.A questão do faturamento ainda está pendente.
5Let's circle back to it on Monday.Vamos voltar a tratar disso na segunda-feira.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Review -- A Global Product Sync

Putting Block 3 together in one global product sync. Review words: directness, rapport, time zone, roadmap, release, feature, feedback, takeaway, align, action item, owner, circle back. Skills: polite directness across cultures · future forms (roadmap) · diplomatic softening (feedback) · reported speech (follow-ups).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Revisão do Bloco 3. Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Directness -- being clear and to the point, but polite (objetividade)
"In the US, directness is normal on a call."
Rapport -- a good, friendly connection with someone (sintonia)
"I build rapport before the meeting starts."
Time zone -- a region with the same standard time (fuso horário)
"We only share two hours across the time zones."
Roadmap -- a plan of what you will build and when (roteiro do produto)
"Let me walk you through the roadmap."
Release -- a new version you deliver to customers (versão/lançamento)
"The next release goes live in May."
Feature -- a function or part of the product (funcionalidade)
"The new reporting feature is almost ready."
Feedback -- comments on what is good and what to improve (feedback)
"Thanks for the feedback on the deck."
Takeaway -- the main point you keep from a meeting (conclusão principal)
"My main takeaway is to simplify the dashboard."
Align -- to agree and move in the same direction (alinhar)
"Let us align on the roadmap first."
Action item -- a task someone must do after a meeting (item de ação)
"Each action item has an owner and a date."
Owner -- the person responsible for a task (responsável)
"Priya is the owner of the training."
Circle back -- to return to a topic again later (retomar depois)
"Let us circle back on the dates by email."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Directness
Rapport
Time zone
Roadmap
Release
Feature
Feedback
Takeaway
Align
Action item
Owner
Circle back

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

Patrícia is getting ready for a global product sync with David and Priya. The team works across three time zones, so she keeps it short. She opens politely: "Could we go over the roadmap first?" Then she shares the plan: "We are launching the new feature in May." David gives some feedback, and she softens her reply: "Perhaps it might be better to simplify the deck." Finally, she reports a follow-up: "Priya said she would book the training." They align on the dates and agree to circle back by email.

1. Por que "We are launching the new feature in May" usa o present continuous?
A Porque a ação terminou no passado.
B Porque é um plano combinado para o futuro.
C Porque é uma rotina de toda semana.
2. "Priya said she would book the training" usa o reported speech porque:
A Relata o que a Priya disse; "will" vira "would".
B É uma pergunta indireta educada.
C É uma ação acontecendo agora.
3. Why does Patrícia say "Perhaps it might be better to simplify the deck"?
A To give an exact deadline.
B To soften the feedback and stay diplomatic.
C To report what someone else said.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Four Skills of Block 3

GRAMMAR

As quatro habilidades que você usa num sync global (explicação em inglês e português).

SkillUse / UsoExample
Polite directness
Could we / Would you...		Claro, mas respeitoso entre culturas. Clear but respectful.Could we go over the roadmap?
Future forms
will / going to / am-is-are + -ing		Planos e o roadmap. Plans and the roadmap.We are launching it in May.
Diplomatic softening
perhaps / it might be better to		Dar feedback com diplomacia. Giving feedback gently.Perhaps it might be better to simplify it.
Reported speech
said / told + backshift (will→would)		Relatar follow-ups e o que ficou combinado. Reporting follow-ups.She said she would book it.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

" go over the roadmap together?"
"We the new feature in May."
"I share the timeline after the call."
"Perhaps it better to simplify the dashboard."
"David said he send the technical notes."

Stage 2: Run the Sync in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de um global sync na ordem correta.

?Report a follow-up: "She said she would book it."
?Open politely and suggest going over the roadmap.
?Align on the dates and agree to circle back by email.
?Share the roadmap: what you are launching and when.
?Handle the feedback diplomatically and give your takeaway.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

Could we go over the roadmap together?
Podemos revisar o roadmap juntos?
We are launching the new feature in May.
Estamos lançando a nova funcionalidade em maio.
Perhaps it might be better to simplify the deck.
Talvez seja melhor simplificar a apresentação.
She said she would book the training.
Ela disse que agendaria o treinamento.
Let us align on the dates and circle back by email.
Vamos alinhar as datas e retomar por e-mail.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

You open the roadmap part of a global sync. The most polite, direct option is:
A "Could we go over the roadmap first?"
B "Go over the roadmap now."
C "Roadmap. Please."
You describe a planned launch. Which is correct?
A "We will launching the feature in May."
B "We are launching the feature in May."
C "We are launch the feature in May."
A teammate's deck is too long. The most diplomatic feedback is:
A "Perhaps it might be better to start with the big picture."
B "Your deck is bad."
C "This deck is wrong, change it."
You report what Priya promised. You say:
A "Priya said she will book the training."
B "Priya said she would book the training."
C "Priya told she would book the training."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine you are running a global product sync. Open politely, share your roadmap (what you are launching and when), handle one piece of feedback diplomatically, and report one follow-up that a teammate agreed to. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Could we go over the roadmap first?Podemos revisar o roadmap primeiro?
2We are launching the new feature in May.Estamos lançando a nova funcionalidade em maio.
3Perhaps it might be better to simplify the deck.Talvez seja melhor simplificar a apresentação.
4She said she would book the training.Ela disse que agendaria o treinamento.
5Let us align and circle back by email.Vamos alinhar e retomar por e-mail.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Presenting Customer Health & Metrics

Reading a customer health report and presenting the numbers. Key words: metric, health score, churn, retention, adoption rate, trend, increase, decrease, compared to, target. Structure: comparatives and trend language (higher/lower than, increased/decreased, a slight/significant rise).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Metric -- a number you measure to track how well something is going (métrica / indicador)
"Three metrics improved this quarter."
Health score -- a single number that shows how healthy a customer account is (pontuação de saúde)
"The overall health score is seventy-eight out of one hundred."
Churn -- the share of customers who stop using or cancel the product (perda de clientes / evasão)
"Churn decreased again, so we are below the target."
Retention -- keeping customers active and using the product over time (retenção)
"Retention is strong: almost all licenses are still active."
Adoption rate -- the percentage of users who actively use the product (taxa de adoção)
"The adoption rate increased to seventy-four percent."
Trend -- the general direction a number moves in over time (tendência)
"The trend over the year is positive."
Increase -- to go up, or a rise in a number (aumentar / aumento)
"The adoption rate increased from sixty to seventy-four percent."
Decrease -- to go down, or a fall in a number (diminuir / queda)
"Churn decreased again this quarter."
Compared to -- used to show the difference between two numbers or times (em comparação com)
"Compared to last quarter, the score is higher."
Target -- the number you plan and aim to reach (meta / alvo)
"Adoption is well above our target of seventy."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Metric
Health score
Churn
Retention
Adoption rate
Trend
Increase
Decrease
Target

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

In the quarterly review, Patrícia presents the metrics. The health score is higher than last quarter, and the adoption rate increased to seventy-four percent — higher than the target of seventy. Churn decreased again, so it is now lower than before. The new dashboard is more efficient than the old report, so the data is easier to read. There is one watch area: support tickets had a significant rise last month, not a small one, and the trend is something the team will follow every week.

1. Para um adjetivo curto como "high", qual forma de comparativo usamos?
A "higher than" (adjetivo curto recebe -er).
B "more high than" (sempre usamos "more").
C "more higher than" (more + -er juntos).
2. Para um adjetivo longo como "efficient", qual forma usamos?
A "efficienter than".
B "more efficient than" (adjetivo longo usa "more").
C "more efficienter than".
3. Which sentence describes a trend correctly?
A "Churn is more low than the target."
B "Churn decreased again this quarter."
C "Churn is gooder than last year."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives & Trends

GRAMMAR

Como comparar números e descrever tendências (explicação em inglês e português).

PatternUse / UsoExample
short adj + -er thanAdjetivo curto (high, low) recebe -er. Nunca "more higher".The score is higher than last quarter.
more + long adj + thanAdjetivo longo (efficient, positive) usa more, não "-er".It is more efficient than the old report.
increased / decreasedVerbos de tendência: o número subiu ou desceu.Adoption increased; churn decreased.
a slight / significant riseSubstantivo para o tamanho da mudança.a slight rise vs a significant rise

Irregulares: good → better, bad → worse. (Nunca "gooder" ou "more good".)

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"The adoption rate is than last quarter."
"Churn is than our target."
"This dashboard is more than the old report."
"Support tickets a lot last month."
"There was a significant in support tickets."

Stage 2: Put the Review in Order

Order

Coloque a apresentação das métricas na ordem correta.

?There is one watch area: support tickets had a significant rise.
?First, the overall health score is higher than last quarter.
?Finally, we will track the trend and compare it to the target.
?The adoption rate increased, well above the target.
?Churn decreased, so it is now lower than before.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

The health score is higher than last quarter.
A pontuação de saúde está mais alta do que no trimestre passado.
The adoption rate increased to seventy-four percent.
A taxa de adoção subiu para setenta e quatro por cento.
Churn is lower than our target.
A evasão está mais baixa do que a nossa meta.
This dashboard is more efficient than the old report.
Este painel é mais eficiente do que o relatório antigo.
There was a significant rise in support tickets.
Houve um aumento significativo nos chamados de suporte.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

The score went from 72 to 78. You present it. You say:
A "The health score is higher than last quarter."
B "The health score is more higher than last quarter."
C "The health score is more high than last quarter."
The new dashboard works better than the old report. You say:
A "It is efficienter than the old report."
B "It is more efficient than the old report."
C "It is more efficienter than the old report."
Tickets went up a lot last month. You describe the trend. You say:
A "There was a slight rise in tickets."
B "There was a significant rise in tickets."
C "Tickets is more high last month."
Retention is good, and it is the best in two years. You say:
A "Retention is more good than before."
B "Retention is better than before."
C "Retention is gooder than before."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Think of a real customer you manage. Present their health in three sentences: give the health score, compare two metrics to the target ("higher than", "lower than"), and describe one trend ("increased", "a significant rise"). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1The health score is higher than last quarter.A pontuação de saúde está mais alta do que no trimestre passado.
2The adoption rate increased to seventy-four percent.A taxa de adoção subiu para setenta e quatro por cento.
3Churn is lower than our target.A evasão está mais baixa do que a nossa meta.
4This dashboard is more efficient than the old report.Este painel é mais eficiente do que o relatório antigo.
5There was a significant rise in support tickets.Houve um aumento significativo nos chamados de suporte.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Negotiating & Managing Expectations

Negotiating scope and deadlines and managing customer expectations. Key words: negotiate, trade-off, compromise, flexible, realistic, commit, scope creep, balance, agreement, reasonable. Structure: second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) and diplomatic modals (we could consider...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Negotiate -- to discuss something to reach an agreement (negociar)
"Let's negotiate a timeline that works for both sides."
Trade-off -- giving up one thing in order to gain another (troca, compensação)
"There's a trade-off between speed and quality."
Compromise -- a solution where each side gives something up (meio-termo, acordo)
"We reached a fair compromise on the scope."
Flexible -- willing to change or adjust your plan (flexível)
"We can be flexible on the timeline."
Realistic -- sensible and possible, not too ambitious (realista)
"Let's set a realistic deadline we can meet."
Commit -- to promise firmly to do something (comprometer-se)
"I can commit to two features, not three."
Scope creep -- extra work added beyond the original plan (aumento gradual do escopo)
"Saying yes to everything causes scope creep."
Balance -- to give fair weight to two different needs (equilibrar)
"We balance what the customer wants and what the team can do."
Agreement -- a decision that both sides accept (acordo)
"We reached an agreement on the next steps."
Reasonable -- fair and sensible, not too much to ask (razoável)
"That sounds like a reasonable request."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Negotiate
Trade-off
Compromise
Flexible
Realistic
Commit
Scope creep
Balance
Agreement
Reasonable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

On the call, David asked Patrícia to commit to three new features before next quarter. Instead of saying no, she negotiated. "If we focused on the most important one first, we would deliver value faster," she said. She explained the trade-off: "If we added all three at once, the timeline would slip." To stay flexible, she offered a phased plan: "We could consider a phased rollout." David agreed, and they set a realistic deadline. "If you gave us two extra weeks, we would commit to all three." It was a fair agreement — and it protected the team from scope creep.

1. Por que Patrícia diz "If we focused on one first, we would deliver faster"?
A Porque ela já entregou tudo.
B Porque imagina um resultado (segunda condicional) e oferece um trade-off.
C Porque está falando de uma rotina diária.
2. Na frase "If we added all three at once, the timeline would slip", a estrutura é:
A if + passado simples, would + verbo base.
B if + would, will + verbo.
C if + present perfect, can + verbo.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "If we would extend it, we would add the feature."
B "If we extended it, we would add the feature."
C "If we extend it, we would added the feature."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

Como imaginar um acordo que ainda não é real e oferecer opções de forma diplomática (explicação em inglês e português).

StructureUse / UsoExample
If + past simple, would + base verbImagina um acordo não real ainda. Imagines a deal not real yet.If we extended it, we would add the feature.
We could consider + noun/-ingOferece uma opção gentilmente. Offers an option gently.We could consider a phased rollout.
We would need + nounColoca uma condição justa. States a fair condition.We would need more time for that.
Would it be possible to + verbo?Pergunta com diplomacia. Asks diplomatically.Would it be possible to start with one?
Avoid / EviteNão use "if I would" na cláusula if → troque por "if I had / if we extended" (passado simples).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"If we on one feature, we would deliver faster."
"If we extended the deadline, we add the feature."
"If I more time, I would say yes."
"We could a phased rollout."
"That sounds like a request."
"That's a realistic, fair ."

Stage 2: Negotiate in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma negociação justa na ordem correta.

?Reach a realistic, fair agreement for both sides.
?Acknowledge the request positively (great goal).
?Protect the deadline and avoid scope creep.
?Offer a trade-off with the second conditional (if..., we would...).
?Suggest a phased option (we could consider...).

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

If we focused on one feature, we would deliver faster.
Se focássemos em um recurso, entregaríamos mais rápido.
There is a trade-off between speed and quality.
Há uma troca entre velocidade e qualidade.
We could consider a phased rollout.
Poderíamos considerar uma implantação em fases.
If you gave us two extra weeks, we would commit to all three.
Se você nos desse duas semanas a mais, nós nos comprometeríamos com os três.
That is a realistic, fair agreement.
Esse é um acordo realista e justo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A customer wants three features at once. You offer a trade-off. You say:
A "No, that is impossible."
B "If we focused on the most important one first, we would deliver value faster."
C "If we would focus on one, we will deliver faster."
The customer pushes for a shorter deadline. You set a fair condition:
A "If you gave us two extra weeks, we would commit to all three."
B "If you would give us two weeks, we commit."
C "Fine, we will do everything tomorrow."
You want to offer an option gently. Which is correct?
A "We could to consider a phased rollout."
B "We could consider a phased rollout."
C "We could considering a phased rollout."
You want to protect the project from extra, unplanned work. You watch out for:
A scope creep, while keeping a realistic deadline.
B saying yes to every request.
C refusing to be flexible at all.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a customer wants more features, sooner. Negotiate: acknowledge the request, offer a trade-off with the second conditional (if..., we would...), suggest a phased option, and reach a fair agreement. Protect the deadline. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1If we focused on one feature, we would deliver faster.Se focássemos em um recurso, entregaríamos mais rápido.
2There is a trade-off between speed and quality.Há uma troca entre velocidade e qualidade.
3We could consider a phased rollout.Poderíamos considerar uma implantação em fases.
4If you gave us two extra weeks, we would commit to all three.Se você nos desse duas semanas a mais, nós nos comprometeríamos com os três.
5That is a realistic, fair agreement.Esse é um acordo realista e justo.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Difficult Conversations & Saying No Politely

Reading a hard customer email and declining a request with tact. Key words: decline, unfortunately, limitation, alternative, honest, reassure, pushback, boundary, tactful, capacity. Structure: hedging / softening a no (I'm afraid..., Unfortunately..., We're not able to...).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Decline -- to politely refuse a request or offer (recusar educadamente)
"We sometimes have to decline a request from a customer."
Unfortunately -- used to introduce bad or negative news politely (infelizmente)
"Unfortunately, we can't offer a discount this time."
Limitation -- a limit on what a product or team is able to do (limitação)
"Every plan has some limitations on support hours."
Alternative -- another option that you offer instead of the first one (alternativa)
"When you say no, always suggest an alternative."
Honest -- telling the truth in a clear way, even when it is hard (honesto / sincero)
"I will be honest with you about the timeline."
Reassure -- to make someone feel calmer and more confident (tranquilizar)
"I want to reassure you that your account matters to us."
Pushback -- resistance or strong disagreement from the other side (resistência / objeção)
"Expect some pushback when you decline a request."
Boundary -- a clear limit you set on what you can or will do (limite)
"Setting a boundary protects both sides."
Tactful -- careful not to upset people; diplomatic and polite (diplomático / com tato)
"A tactful no keeps the relationship strong."
Capacity -- the amount of work a team is able to handle (capacidade)
"We don't have the capacity for 24/7 support."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Decline
Unfortunately
Limitation
Alternative
Honest
Reassure
Pushback
Boundary
Tactful
Capacity

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

When Patrícia has to say no, she never starts with a flat "no". First, she thanks the customer and is honest about the limitation. Then she softens the message. I'm afraid we can't deliver it by Friday. Unfortunately, a thirty percent discount is not possible. We're not able to provide 24/7 support at this time. I understand, but the timeline is fixed. After the no, she always offers an alternative and reassures the customer.

1. Por que a Patrícia evita começar a resposta com um "no" seco?
A Porque "no" não existe em inglês formal.
B Porque soa frio e gera mais resistência; o ideal é suavizar primeiro.
C Porque ela nunca pode recusar nada a um cliente.
2. Qual expressão usamos para suavizar uma má notícia ANTES de dizer não?
A "I'm afraid we can't..." (Receio que não possamos...).
B "I'm happy we can't...".
C "No problem, we can't...".
3. Which reply is the most polite and professional?
A "No. That is impossible."
B "Unfortunately, that isn't possible, but here is an alternative."
C "We can't. Discount no."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Softening a No (Hedging)

GRAMMAR

Como dizer "não" com tato em inglês (explicação em inglês e português).

SoftenerUse / UsoExample
I'm afraid...Suaviza a má notícia antes do não. "Receio que..."I'm afraid we can't do that.
Unfortunately,Introduz a resposta negativa com tato (+ vírgula). "Infelizmente,"Unfortunately, that isn't possible.
We're not able to...Um "we can't" mais suave. "Não conseguimos..."We're not able to offer that at this time.
I understand, but...Mostra empatia e então dá o limite. "Eu entendo, mas..."I understand, but the timeline is fixed.

Regra de ouro: nunca comece com um "No." seco. Suavize primeiro, depois ofereça uma alternativa. "At this time" faz o não soar temporário, não definitivo.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"I'm we can't deliver it by Friday."
", a discount is not possible."
"We're not to offer that at this time."
"I understand, the timeline is fixed."
"Let me suggest an that works for both sides."

Stage 2: Put the Polite Reply in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma resposta educada de "não" na ordem correta.

?Offer an alternative the customer can accept.
?Thank the customer and acknowledge the request.
?Reassure them that their account matters, and confirm next steps.
?Be honest about the limitation.
?Say no politely with "I'm afraid" or "Unfortunately".

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

I'm afraid we can't deliver it by Friday.
Receio que não consigamos entregar até sexta-feira.
Unfortunately, that discount isn't possible.
Infelizmente, esse desconto não é possível.
We're not able to offer that at this time.
Não conseguimos oferecer isso no momento.
I understand, but the timeline is fixed.
Eu entendo, mas o prazo é fixo.
Let me suggest an alternative that works for both sides.
Deixe-me sugerir uma alternativa que funcione para os dois lados.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

A customer asks for a feature you can't deliver this week. You say:
A "No. Impossible this week."
B "I'm afraid we can't deliver it this week, but I can give you a firm date."
C "Maybe. We see."
A customer demands a big discount you cannot offer. You say:
A "Unfortunately, a discount that size isn't possible, but I can add premium support."
B "Discount no. Never."
C "You are wrong to ask that."
You want to refuse 24/7 support softly, leaving the door open. You say:
A "We can't. End of story."
B "We're not able to provide that at this time, but here is an alternative."
C "That is not my problem."
The customer is frustrated. Before the bad news, you want to calm them. You:
A ignore their feelings and just say no.
B reassure them that their account matters, then give the honest answer.
C tell them to escalate if they don't like it.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Think of a real request you had to decline at work. Say no politely: thank the customer, be honest about the limitation, use "I'm afraid" or "Unfortunately", offer an alternative, and reassure them. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1I'm afraid we can't deliver it by Friday.Receio que não consigamos entregar até sexta-feira.
2Unfortunately, that discount isn't possible.Infelizmente, esse desconto não é possível.
3We're not able to offer that at this time.Não conseguimos oferecer isso no momento.
4I understand, but the timeline is fixed.Eu entendo, mas o prazo é fixo.
5Let me suggest an alternative that works for both sides.Deixe-me sugerir uma alternativa que funcione para os dois lados.
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Renewals & Driving Value

Demonstrating the value you've delivered, justifying a contract renewal, and proposing an upsell. Key words: renewal, contract, ROI, demonstrate, growth, upsell, retain, justify, partnership, track record. Structure: present perfect for results (have/has + past participle, since/for) and persuasion (that's why / this means).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

Renewal -- continuing a contract for another period (renovação)
"The contract renewal is coming up next month."
Contract -- a formal written agreement between two sides (contrato)
"Let's review the contract before we renew."
ROI -- return on investment; the value you get back (retorno sobre o investimento)
"The ROI on this partnership is clear."
Demonstrate -- to show clearly that something is true (demonstrar)
"Let me demonstrate the value we've delivered."
Growth -- an increase in size, number, or value (crescimento)
"Adoption growth has been strong this year."
Upsell -- to offer a customer a higher or extra plan (venda adicional)
"After we show value, we can suggest an upsell."
Retain -- to keep a customer with you (reter)
"Our goal is to retain happy customers."
Justify -- to give a good reason for something (justificar)
"These results justify the renewal."
Partnership -- a relationship where two sides work together (parceria)
"This partnership has delivered real value."
Track record -- a history of past results and performance (histórico de resultados)
"We have a strong track record of results."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

Renewal
Contract
ROI
Demonstrate
Growth
Upsell
Retain
Justify
Partnership
Track record

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

The contract was up for renewal, and David's finance team wanted proof. So Patrícia led with results. "We have grown adoption by forty percent since January," she said, "and your team has saved many hours." The partnership has delivered real value for six months. She used the present perfect because the results were still true today. The ROI was clear, and the track record spoke for itself — that was enough to justify the renewal. David was ready to renew. Then, because growth had been so strong, Patrícia suggested an upsell to help him retain that momentum.

1. Por que Patrícia diz "We have grown adoption since January" no present perfect?
A Porque o resultado terminou e não vale mais.
B Porque é um resultado que continua verdadeiro hoje.
C Porque é um plano para o futuro.
2. Na frase "the partnership has delivered value for six months", usamos "for" porque:
A "for" indica um período de tempo (seis meses).
B "for" indica um ponto no tempo (janeiro).
C "for" só aparece no passado simples.
3. Which sentence is correct?
A "We grew adoption by 40% since January."
B "We've grown adoption by 40% since January."
C "We've grown adoption since six months."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect for Results

GRAMMAR

Como mostrar um resultado que ainda é verdadeiro hoje e transformá-lo em persuasão (explicação em inglês e português).

StructureUse / UsoExample
have / has + past participleResultado ainda verdadeiro agora. A result still true now.We have grown adoption.
since + ponto no tempoQuando o resultado começou. When it started....since January.
for + período de tempoHá quanto tempo dura. How long it has lasted....for six months.
That's why... / This means...Transforma resultado em persuasão. Turns a result into persuasion.The ROI is clear. That's why renewing makes sense.
Avoid / EviteNão use o passado simples com "since" ("we grew... since January") → troque por "we've grown... since January". E não use o present perfect com tempo terminado ("last quarter") → use o passado simples ali.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase com a forma correta. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"We grown adoption by forty percent."
"Your team saved many hours this year."
"We've reduced churn January."
"The partnership has delivered value six months."
"These results the renewal."
"The ROI is clear, so I'd recommend an ."

Stage 2: Renew in Order

Order

Coloque os passos de uma renovação focada em valor na ordem correta.

?Confirm the contract renewal with the customer.
?Lead with results in the present perfect (we've grown..., has saved...).
?Propose an upsell to retain the growth.
?Show the ROI and the track record.
?Justify the renewal (that's why renewing makes sense).

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

We have grown adoption by forty percent since January.
Nós aumentamos a adoção em quarenta por cento desde janeiro.
Your team has saved many hours this year.
Sua equipe economizou muitas horas este ano.
The partnership has delivered real value.
A parceria entregou valor real.
The ROI is clear, so these results justify the renewal.
O retorno é claro, então esses resultados justificam a renovação.
Because growth has been strong, I would recommend an upsell.
Como o crescimento foi forte, eu recomendaria uma venda adicional.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

The customer asks what value they got this year. You lead with results:
A "We grew adoption since January."
B "We've grown adoption by forty percent since January."
C "We've grown adoption last quarter."
You describe how long the partnership has delivered value. You say:
A "It has delivered value for six months."
B "It has delivered value since six months."
C "It delivered value for now."
You want to justify the renewal to finance. The strongest line is:
A "Please just trust me on the price."
B "The ROI is clear; our track record speaks for itself."
C "The contract is cheap, so renew it."
When is the right moment to suggest an upsell?
A After you have demonstrated the value and the ROI.
B Before you show any results.
C Only if the customer complains.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Imagine a contract is up for renewal. Demonstrate the value: say three results in the present perfect (we've grown / has saved / has delivered) with since/for, justify the renewal with the ROI and your track record, and propose an upsell. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1We have grown adoption by forty percent since January.Nós aumentamos a adoção em quarenta por cento desde janeiro.
2Your team has saved many hours this year.Sua equipe economizou muitas horas este ano.
3The partnership has delivered real value.A parceria entregou valor real.
4The ROI is clear, so these results justify the renewal.O retorno é claro, então esses resultados justificam a renovação.
5Because growth has been strong, I would recommend an upsell.Como o crescimento foi forte, eu recomendaria uma venda adicional.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

Capstone -- Quarterly Business Review (QBR)

The final lesson: reading a real QBR executive summary and running your own review. Key words: QBR, summary, highlight, recommendation, objective, stakeholder, achievement, roadmap, align, next quarter. Toolkit: present perfect, comparatives, reported speech and conditionals together.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Ouça cada palavra e leia o exemplo. Toque em Listen para escutar.

QBR -- Quarterly Business Review: encontro periódico para revisar os resultados do cliente e planejar o próximo trimestre (revisão trimestral de negócios)
"We open every QBR by looking back at the results."
Summary -- um relato curto que traz apenas os pontos principais (resumo)
"The executive summary fits the whole quarter on one page."
Highlight -- o melhor resultado que você quer que as pessoas percebam (destaque)
"The biggest highlight is the jump in adoption."
Recommendation -- uma sugestão clara sobre o que fazer a seguir (recomendação)
"Our main recommendation is a short training for finance."
Objective -- um resultado claro que você planeja e busca (objetivo)
"We set three objectives for next quarter."
Stakeholder -- pessoa com interesse no sucesso da conta (parte interessada) — revisão
"I present the QBR to the main stakeholders."
Achievement -- algo bom que você conquistou com esforço (conquista) — revisão
"Resolving every critical ticket on time was a real achievement."
Roadmap -- um plano que mostra o que vem e quando (roteiro) — revisão
"The roadmap shows two new features next quarter."
Align -- levar as pessoas ao mesmo entendimento ou objetivo (alinhar) — revisão
"A QBR helps us align on what matters most."
Next quarter -- o período de três meses que vem depois deste (próximo trimestre)
"We will review the progress again next quarter."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Relacione cada palavra com a definição correta.

QBR
Summary
Highlight
Recommendation
Objective
Stakeholder
Achievement
Roadmap
Align
Next quarter

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Leia o texto e responda às perguntas.

At the end of each quarter, Patrícia runs a QBR with her customers. She has prepared the summary in advance, so the highlights are clear. Compared to last quarter, the adoption rate is higher, and the support team has resolved every critical ticket. In the last call, the customer said that the new walkthrough had saved a lot of time. There is one honest concern, so Patrícia adds a recommendation: if finance gets more training, their adoption will catch up. Finally, she aligns with the stakeholders on the objectives for next quarter.

1. Por que dizemos "adoption has grown" e não "adoption grew" num resumo de resultados?
A Porque "grew" não existe em inglês.
B Porque o present perfect mostra o resultado até agora, sem dizer exatamente quando.
C Porque o present perfect é sempre futuro.
2. No reported speech: "The customer said the walkthrough ___ time." Qual forma?
A "had saved" (o verbo recua — backshift).
B "has saved" (sem mudar nada).
C "will save".
3. Which sentence is correct (first conditional)?
A "If finance will get more training, adoption will improve."
B "If finance gets more training, adoption will improve."
C "If finance gets more training, adoption improve."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The QBR Toolkit

GRAMMAR

Uma QBR usa as estruturas de todo o curso ao mesmo tempo (explicação em inglês e português).

StructureUse / UsoExample
Present perfectresultados até agora, sem dizer quando.Adoption has grown.
Comparativescomparar com o trimestre anterior.higher than last quarter
Reported speechcitar o cliente; o verbo recua (is → was).She said it had saved time.
First conditionalum plano real: if + presente, will + verbo.If they get support, adoption will catch up.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete cada frase. Toque em Listen para ouvir a frase inteira.

"This quarter, adoption by twenty percent."
"The health score is than last quarter."
"He said the team resolved every ticket."
"If finance more training, adoption will improve."
"We will on the objectives for next quarter."

Stage 2: Put the QBR in Order

Order

Coloque as partes de uma QBR na ordem correta.

?Once the concern is clear, give your recommendation.
?First, present the highlights of the quarter.
?Finally, align on the objectives for next quarter.
?Then, share the metrics compared to last quarter.
?After that, raise the one honest concern.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Ouça cada frase e depois grave você mesma dizendo-a.

This quarter, adoption has grown across all teams.
Neste trimestre, a adoção cresceu em todas as equipes.
The adoption rate is higher than last quarter.
A taxa de adoção está mais alta que no trimestre passado.
I recommend a short extra training for finance.
Eu recomendo um treinamento extra curto para o financeiro.
If they get more support, their adoption will catch up.
Se receberem mais apoio, a adoção deles vai alcançar.
The partnership has delivered real value.
A parceria entregou valor de verdade.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Escolha a melhor resposta para cada situação real de trabalho.

You open the QBR with your strongest result. You say:
A "The biggest highlight is that adoption has grown to eighty percent."
B "Let me start with the agenda and the small details."
C "Adoption grew, but I am not sure when."
You have one weak area and want to stay honest but helpful. You say:
A "Everything is perfect, no problems at all."
B "There is one honest concern, and my recommendation is a short extra training."
C "Finance is a disaster and it is their fault."
You quote what the customer told you last week. You say:
A "The customer says the walkthrough saves a lot of time."
B "The customer said the walkthrough had saved a lot of time."
C "The customer said the walkthrough will saved time."
You close the QBR by agreeing on what comes next. You say:
A "Let's align on the objectives for next quarter."
B "Let's align on the objectives for last quarter."
C "Let's not make any plan."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Grave você mesma respondendo à pergunta abaixo. Não há resposta certa ou errada.

Think of a real account you manage. Give a short QBR: present one highlight (present perfect), compare it to last quarter (comparative), raise one honest concern, and add a recommendation with a first conditional. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1This quarter, adoption has grown across all teams.Neste trimestre, a adoção cresceu em todas as equipes.
2The adoption rate is higher than last quarter.A taxa de adoção está mais alta que no trimestre passado.
3There is one honest concern, and here is my recommendation.Há uma preocupação honesta, e aqui está minha recomendação.
4If they get more support, their adoption will catch up.Se receberem mais apoio, a adoção deles vai alcançar.
5Let's align on the objectives for next quarter.Vamos alinhar os objetivos para o próximo trimestre.

IN CLASS -- Selecione a Aula

01
Introducing Yourself & Your Role
A first call with a new global colleague -- 27 slides
02
Talking About Your Experience
Sharing your career and background with a new colleague -- 27 slides
03
Status Updates & Ongoing Work
Giving a clear project update on a call -- 27 slides
04
Asking Effective Questions on Calls
Asking clear questions to get the answers you need -- 27 slides
05
Review — A Customer Check-in Call
Putting Block 1 together in one real call -- 27 slides
06
Onboarding & Driving Adoption
Reading a customer success plan + sequencing words -- 28 slides
07
Understanding Customer Needs
Listening for needs and recommending the right solution -- 27 slides
08
Handling a Technical Issue
Reading an incident report + present perfect for results -- 28 slides
09
Escalations Under Pressure
First conditional + urgency for critical escalations -- 27 slides
10
Review -- Resolving a Critical Escalation
Reading a critical escalation case study + Block 2 review -- 28 slides
11
Working Across Cultures (India/US)
Polite directness and clear communication with global teams -- 27 slides
12
Product Calls & Roadmap
Reading a product roadmap + future forms -- 28 slides
13
Giving & Receiving Feedback
Diplomatic softening for honest, kind feedback -- 27 slides
14
Follow-ups & Action Items
Reading meeting minutes + reported speech -- 28 slides
15
Review — A Global Product Sync
Putting Block 3 together in one global sync -- 27 slides
16
Presenting Customer Health & Metrics
Reading a customer health report + comparatives and trends -- 28 slides
17
Negotiating & Managing Expectations
Second conditional and diplomatic modals to negotiate scope and deadlines -- 27 slides
18
Difficult Conversations & Saying No Politely
Reading a hard customer email + saying no with tact -- 28 slides
19
Renewals & Driving Value
Present perfect for results and persuasion to demonstrate value and renew the contract -- 27 slides
20
Capstone — Quarterly Business Review (QBR)
Reading a real QBR executive summary + consolidating every structure -- 28 slides

Atividades Complementares

Materiais extras para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído após assistir/ouvir.

Lesson 1 — Introducing Yourself & Your Role

Series
Modern Family -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

The characters introduce themselves and explain who they are and what they are doing right now — perfect natural present simple vs present continuous. You already love this show, so use it to learn. Connection to Lesson 1: real self-introductions and the difference between routine ("I work...") and current actions ("I'm doing...").

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 times a character says what they are doing right now (am/is/are + verb-ing).

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Introductions & Talking About Your Job"

Short, clear episodes about real professional English. Listen to the natural phrases people use on a first call to say their role, what they are responsible for, and what they are working on. Connection to Lesson 1: the exact expressions you need with your global teams.

Tip: listen twice. Write down 5 phrases you can use to introduce yourself and your role on a call.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Present Simple vs Present Continuous" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on the exact contrast from today: routine duties (present simple) versus what is happening now (present continuous). Connection to Lesson 1: reinforces the grammar that will make your status updates correct and confident on every call.

Tip: pause and say one sentence about your routine and one about your current project after each example.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Talking About Your Experience

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Mike walks into a job interview and has to talk about his background, his experience and what he has done so far — under pressure. Listen for how characters mix the Past Simple (finished, dated facts) with the Present Perfect (experience up to now). Connection to Lesson 2: describing your career and achievements with confidence.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences where someone talks about past experience, and mark whether they used Past Simple or Present Perfect.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Talking About Your Career & Work Experience"

Short, clear episodes with the exact phrases people use to describe their background and achievements on a call or in an interview. Hear the natural difference between "I worked there for five years" (finished) and "I have worked here since 2019" (up to now). Connection to Lesson 2: the language you need to present your experience to global teams.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 phrases you could use to describe your own career and experience.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Present Perfect vs Past Simple" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's contrast: the Present Perfect for experience and unfinished time (for, since, so far) versus the Past Simple for finished, dated actions (in 2019, last year). Connection to Lesson 2: this is the grammar that will make you talk about your experience without hesitation.

Tip: pause and say one Past Simple sentence (a dated fact) and one Present Perfect sentence (experience so far) about your own career after each example.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Status Updates & Ongoing Work

Series
Silicon Valley -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Minimum Viable Product"

The team runs short stand-up meetings where everyone reports what they are working on, what is blocked, and what is on track. Listen for how they use the Present Continuous ("we are building...", "it is running...") to describe ongoing work. Connection to Lesson 3: giving a clear status update and naming blockers and priorities.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences where someone says what they are doing right now, and underline the am/is/are + verb-ing.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Project Updates & Reporting Progress"

Short, clear episodes with the exact phrases people use to give a status update on a call: reporting progress, flagging a blocker, and naming the priority for the week. Hear the natural difference between "we are testing it now" (happening now) and "we test it every release" (routine). Connection to Lesson 3: the language of updates, milestones, and ongoing work.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 phrases you could use to update your manager on the rollout you are running now.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Present Continuous vs Present Simple" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's contrast: the Present Continuous for what is happening now (right now, at the moment, currently) versus the Present Simple for routines and facts (every Friday, usually, always). Connection to Lesson 3: this is the grammar that makes your status updates sound correct and confident.

Tip: pause and say one Present Continuous sentence (what you are doing now) and one Present Simple sentence (a weekly routine) about your own work after each example.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Asking Effective Questions on Calls

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 5, Episode 14: "Stress Relief"

Watch the meeting scenes where people clarify, confirm, and raise concerns. Listen for polite, indirect questions like "Could you tell me..." and "Do you know if..." that soften a request on a call or in a room. Connection to Lesson 4: asking effective questions to get a clear answer without sounding blunt.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 questions someone asks, and rewrite each one as an indirect question ("Could you tell me what...?", "Do you know if...?").

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Asking & Clarifying Questions in Meetings"

Short, practical episodes with the exact phrases you use to ask for information and clarify on a call: "Could you tell me...", "Do you know if...", "I was wondering...", and "Just to double-check...". Hear how a polite indirect question keeps the statement word order ("...what the status is", not "...what is the status"). Connection to Lesson 4: asking effective questions to confirm details and address concerns.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 indirect questions you could use to clarify an action item with a global team.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Indirect Questions -- Polite English" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: how to turn a direct question ("What is the status?") into a polite indirect one ("Could you tell me what the status is?"). Watch for the word-order change and the use of "if/whether" for yes-no questions. Connection to Lesson 4: this is the grammar that makes your questions on calls sound polite and correct.

Tip: pause after each example and say the direct question first, then the indirect version out loud, using a real question you need to ask your team this week.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Review — A Customer Check-in Call

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 2, Episode 1: "The Dundies" (client & team check-ins)

Watch any scene where a manager runs a quick check-in with a client or the team: someone gives an update on what is happening now, mentions what they have already done, and asks a question. Notice the natural mix of Present Continuous ("we are working on..."), Present Perfect ("I have finished...") and polite questions. Connection to Lesson 5: this is exactly the four-structure mix you used on the call with Mark.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down one sentence for each structure you reviewed today: now, routine, experience, and a question.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Customer Check-in Calls & Account Reviews"

Short, practical episodes built around the exact call you practiced today: opening with the agenda, giving a status update, reporting what is done and what is pending, and closing with a polite question. Hear how proficient speakers move between the four Block 1 structures inside one real customer conversation. Connection to Lesson 5: the full language of a customer success check-in.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 phrases you could use to open and close your next check-in with a real account.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Present Perfect vs Past Simple vs Present Continuous" -- BBC Learning English

A clear review of three of the four structures you consolidated today: the Present Continuous for what is happening now, the Present Perfect for experience and results, and the Past Simple for finished actions with an exact time. Connection to Lesson 5: these are the tenses that make your check-in calls sound correct and confident.

Tip: after each example, say one sentence about your own work: one in the Present Continuous (now), one in the Present Perfect (experience), and one in the Past Simple (last week).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Onboarding & Driving Adoption

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Watch how a new person is welcomed and walked through their first day at work. Listen for the step-by-step language ("first", "then", "after that") and the moments where someone explains a process to a newcomer. Connection to Lesson 6: onboarding someone and guiding them through the first steps so they feel comfortable.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 steps anyone explains, and rewrite them with "first", "then" and "after that".

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Gain Grow Retain Podcast -- "Customer Onboarding & Driving Adoption"

A Customer Success podcast with episodes on exactly your job: onboarding new customers, naming a champion, and driving adoption so customers see real value. Listen for the words from this lesson (enable, walkthrough, configure, success plan, best practice) used by real CS managers. Connection to Lesson 6: building a success plan that turns a new customer into an active, happy user.

Tip: pick one episode and note 5 sequencing phrases ("first...", "then...", "once...") you could use on your next kickoff call.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Sequencing Words -- First, Then, Next, After That, Finally" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: the words we use to describe a process in order ("first", "then", "after that", "once", "finally"). Watch for the comma after the connector and the present tense after "once". Connection to Lesson 6: this is the grammar that lets you walk a customer through their onboarding step by step.

Tip: pause after each example and describe one step of your own onboarding out loud, using the sequencing word you just heard.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Understanding Customer Needs

Series
Silicon Valley -- Season 1, Episode 5: "Signaling Risk"

Watch the pitch and sales scenes where the team tries to understand what a client really needs and recommend a fit. Listen for how people present an option, a benefit, and a solution. Connection to Lesson 7: hearing the customer's need and recommending the right solution with "you should" and "I'd recommend".

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 needs a character mentions, and turn each into a recommendation ("You should...", "I'd recommend...").

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Gain Grow Retain Podcast -- "Running a Great Discovery Call"

A Customer Success podcast about exactly today's skill: asking discovery questions to uncover a customer's real needs, then recommending the right solution. Hear the language of needs, requirements, options, and benefits used by real CSMs. Connection to Lesson 7: understanding needs and making a clear recommendation.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 phrases the hosts use to recommend something ("I'd recommend...", "Why don't you...", "You could...").

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Giving Advice & Recommendations -- should, could, why don't you" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: how to make a recommendation with "should", "could", "I'd recommend", and "why don't you". Watch for the base verb after the modal (no "to") and the "-ing" form after "recommend". Connection to Lesson 7: this is the grammar that makes your recommendations on calls sound clear and correct.

Tip: pause after each example and say it out loud, using a real recommendation you need to make to a customer this week.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Handling a Technical Issue

Series
The IT Crowd -- Season 1, Episode 1 (technical support & ticket talk)

A comedy set inside a tech support team. Watch any scene where they take a call about a problem: someone reports an issue, the team asks questions, and reports what they have done. Listen for the language of bugs, tickets and fixes — the same words you used in this lesson. Connection to Lesson 8: handling a technical issue and reporting the result.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences in the present perfect ("they have fixed...", "we have checked...") about what the team did.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Dealing with Problems & Giving Status Updates"

Short, practical episodes on exactly the call you practiced: explaining a problem, reporting what has been done, and giving a clear status update to a customer. Hear how proficient speakers use the present perfect to report results inside a real support conversation. Connection to Lesson 8: the language of handling a technical issue.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 phrases you could use to report progress on a real ticket at work.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Present Perfect for Results & Recent Actions" -- BBC Learning English

A clear review of the grammar you discovered today: using the present perfect ("we have fixed it", "the issue has not come back") to report a result that matters now, and the difference from the past simple with an exact time. Connection to Lesson 8: the tense that makes your status updates sound correct and confident.

Tip: after each example, say one sentence about your work: one result in the present perfect ("we have resolved...") and one finished action in the past simple ("we fixed it yesterday").

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Escalations Under Pressure

Series
The Morning Show -- Season 1, Episode 7: "Open Waters"

A live crisis blows up and the team has to escalate fast, manage impact, and make commitments under real pressure. Listen for how people stay calm, set priorities, and promise next steps. Connection to Lesson 9: handling a critical situation with clear, confident language.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences where someone reacts to an urgent problem, and mark the first conditional ("If... we will...").

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Dealing with an Angry Customer & Urgent Problems"

Short, clear episodes with the exact phrases you use to acknowledge impact, set severity, and promise updates when something is critical. Hear the natural first conditional ("If we don't fix it, we'll...") and urgency words like unless and as soon as. Connection to Lesson 9: the language you need to escalate calmly on a call.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 phrases you could use to reassure a customer during an escalation.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"First Conditional (If + present + will)" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: the first conditional for real future results, plus how the time clause after when / as soon as / unless stays in the present. Connection to Lesson 9: this is the grammar that lets you explain a plan and make commitments under pressure.

Tip: pause and say one first-conditional sentence about a critical call ("If the system goes down, I will...") after each example.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — Review -- Resolving a Critical Escalation

Series
The IT Crowd -- a "system is down" crisis episode (escalation under pressure)

Pick any episode where the whole system goes down and the team is under pressure. Watch how they handle a critical outage: who takes ownership, what they try first, and how they update the people waiting. It is a fun mirror of the escalation you reviewed today. Connection to Lesson 10: resolving a critical escalation, start to finish.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write three sentences about the crisis using Block 2 words (escalation, impact, workaround, resolve).

Watch ↗
Podcast
Gain Grow Retain -- a customer success episode on managing escalations

A practical customer success podcast. Find an episode about handling a difficult account or a critical escalation, and hear how senior CS managers keep customers calm, set commitments, and follow up. The exact skill you reviewed in this lesson, from real practitioners. Connection to Lesson 10: communicating clearly and calmly during a critical escalation.

Tip: listen twice. Note five phrases you could use to set a commitment with a stressed customer.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"First Conditional + Present Perfect" -- BBC Learning English review

A clear review of the two grammar tools you use most in an escalation: the present perfect for results ("we have resolved it") and the first conditional for commitments ("if it comes back, we will call you"). Watch it to consolidate the Block 2 toolkit before you move on. Connection to Lesson 10: the grammar that makes your escalation updates correct and confident.

Tip: after each example, say one sentence about your work: one result in the present perfect and one commitment in the first conditional.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 11 — Working Across Cultures (India/US)

Series
Outsourced (2010) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

An American manager moves to run a team in India and learns, scene by scene, how directness, etiquette, and norms differ between the two cultures. Watch how small misunderstandings happen and how building rapport fixes them. Connection to Lesson 11: adapting your communication style to work well across cultures.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down two moments where being too direct caused a problem, and rewrite the line as a softer, more polite request.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Cross-Cultural Communication on Global Teams"

Practical episodes on how to adapt your tone for international colleagues: when to be direct, how to soften a request, and how to build rapport across time zones. Hear the exact phrases that prevent a misunderstanding, like "Would it be okay if..." and "When you get a chance...". Connection to Lesson 11: polite directness with a global team.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 softening phrases you could use this week with your India or US colleagues.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"How to Make Polite Requests in English" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on today's grammar: turning a direct order ("Send it now") into a polite request ("Could you possibly send it?", "Would you mind sending it?", "Would it be okay if..."). Watch for how the softer forms keep you clear but never blunt. Connection to Lesson 11: the language of polite directness that works across cultures.

Tip: pause after each example. Say the direct order first, then the polite version out loud, using a real request you need to make to a global colleague.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Product Calls & Roadmap

Series
Silicon Valley (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Minimum Viable Product"

A small tech team plans what to build first and what to leave for later. Listen for how they talk about features, releases and roadmaps, and how they decide what to prioritize.

Watch with English subtitles. Write down three sentences about future plans, then rewrite each one in a different future form ("we are going to build" / "we are launching" / "it will help").

Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Talking About Future Plans & Projects"

A clear episode on talking about plans at work: announcing what you are going to deliver, giving dates for a launch, and managing what stays for later, all with will, going to and the present continuous.

Record a 90-second roadmap of your own product or project: one release you are going to deliver, one launch with a date, and one request that is still on the backlog.

YouTube
BBC Learning English -- "will, going to & present continuous for the future"
