A focused lesson on the three future forms and when to use each one: predictions and promises, decided plans, and fixed arrangements. Directly reinforces today's grammar discovery on talking about a roadmap.

After watching, write six sentences about your next quarter, mixing a release, a launch with a date and a backlog request. Read them aloud.

Lesson 13 — Giving & Receiving Feedback

Series
Ted Lasso -- Season 1, Episode 8: "The Diamond Dogs"

Ted gives and receives tough feedback without ever losing warmth. Listen for how characters lead with a strength, soften criticism, and keep the tone kind. Connection to Lesson 13: giving constructive feedback and receiving it well, the diplomatic way.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 moments where someone softens feedback instead of attacking. Mark the softener they use (perhaps, maybe, it might be better to).

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Giving & Receiving Feedback at Work"

Short, clear episodes with the exact phrases people use to soften feedback on a call: perhaps, it might be better to, I'd suggest, have you considered. You also hear how to receive feedback well — acknowledge, appreciate, and agree a takeaway. Connection to Lesson 13: the diplomatic language for your weekly 1:1s and customer calls.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 softening phrases and 3 ways to respond gracefully when you receive feedback.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Diplomatic English: How to Soften Your Language" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: softeners like perhaps, maybe, it might be better to, and turning direct statements into polite suggestions and questions. Connection to Lesson 13: this is the language that makes your feedback honest and kind at the same time.

Tip: pause and rewrite each direct sentence as a soft one. Then say one strength and one softened area for improvement about a recent call of your own.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Follow-ups & Action Items

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 2, Episode 21: "Conflict Resolution"

Watch a meeting where the team lists problems and decides who will handle what. Listen for the action items, the owners, and the moments when one person reports what another person said. Connection to Lesson 14: turning a meeting into clear tasks and reporting decisions to the team.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 things people decide, then report each one with "She said..." or "He told the team...".

Watch ↗
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- "How to Run Better Meetings (and Follow Up on Them)"

A Harvard Business Review episode on running meetings that end with clear action items, owners and next steps -- and on the follow-up that makes them stick. Listen for the words from this lesson (action item, owner, due, outstanding, wrap up) used by real managers. Connection to Lesson 14: making sure a meeting turns into work that actually gets done.

Tip: pick one episode and note 5 phrases you could use to wrap up your own sync and assign the action items.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Reported Speech -- Say & Tell + Backshift" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: how to report what people said with "say" and "tell" and how to shift the verb back in time (is → was, will → would, have → had). Watch for why "tell" needs a person and "say" does not. Connection to Lesson 14: this is the grammar that lets you recap a meeting and report what your team agreed.

Tip: pause after each example and report one thing a colleague said to you today, using "said" or "told" with the correct backshift.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Review — A Global Product Sync

Series
The Morning Show -- Any global team meeting scene (Apple TV+)

Watch a scene where a multicultural team runs a planning or product meeting: someone presents what is coming next, another gives careful feedback, and they agree on next steps. Notice the polite-but-direct openings ("Could we...?"), the future forms for plans ("we are launching..."), and how people soften criticism. Connection to Lesson 15: this is exactly the four-skill mix you used on the global sync with David and Priya.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote uma frase para cada habilidade: directness, roadmap/future, feedback softening, e um follow-up reportado.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Working with Global & Cross-Cultural Teams"

Short, practical episodes built around the exact call you practiced today: opening a sync across time zones, walking a team through the roadmap, giving and receiving feedback diplomatically, and reporting follow-ups. Hear how proficient speakers move between the four Block 3 skills inside one real global meeting. Connection to Lesson 15: the full language of a global product sync.

Dica: ouça duas vezes. Anote 5 frases para abrir e fechar o seu próximo sync global com action items e owners.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Reported Speech & Future Forms for Meetings" -- BBC Learning English

A clear review of two of the four skills you consolidated today: future forms for talking about the roadmap (will / going to / present continuous for arranged plans), and reported speech for following up on what people agreed (will → would). Connection to Lesson 15: these are the structures that make your global syncs sound correct and confident.

Dica: depois de cada exemplo, diga uma frase sobre o seu próprio trabalho: uma com future form (o roadmap) e uma com reported speech (um follow-up).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — Presenting Customer Health & Metrics

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 2, Episode 21: "Conflict Resolution"

Watch a workplace "review" where someone presents numbers and problems to a group. Listen for how people compare results and react to good and bad news. Connection to Lesson 16: presenting metrics and a watch area to stakeholders, and comparing one number to another.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences where someone compares two things, then rewrite them with "higher than" or "more efficient than".

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Gain Grow Retain Podcast -- "Customer Health Scores & Metrics That Matter"

A Customer Success podcast for your exact job: building health scores, tracking adoption and churn, and presenting metrics in a QBR. Listen for the words from this lesson (metric, health score, churn, retention, adoption rate, trend, target) used by real CS leaders. Connection to Lesson 16: turning a dashboard of numbers into a clear story for a customer.

Tip: pick one episode and note 5 comparison phrases ("higher than...", "increased to...", "lower than the target...") you could use in your next review.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Comparatives and Superlatives -- Describing Trends & Numbers" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: comparatives (higher than, more efficient than) and the language of trends (increase, decrease, a slight/significant rise). Watch for the "-er" vs "more" rule and the irregular forms (good → better). Connection to Lesson 16: this is the grammar that lets you present and compare your customer metrics.

Tip: pause after each example and describe one of your own metrics out loud, comparing it to the target with the structure you just heard.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Negotiating & Managing Expectations

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Lawyers negotiate deals all day long. Listen for how they offer trade-offs, set conditions, and reach an agreement instead of simply saying no. Connection to Lesson 17: negotiating scope and managing expectations with diplomatic, conditional language.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 moments where someone offers a trade-off ("if you... I would..."). Mark how they protect their own position.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Negotiation English: Bargaining & Making Concessions"

Short, clear episodes with the exact phrases people use to negotiate scope and timelines: if-conditionals, "we could consider...", "we would need...", and "would it be possible to...". You also hear how to manage expectations without saying a flat no. Connection to Lesson 17: the language for your customer negotiation calls.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 conditional sentences (if + past, would + base) and 3 ways to offer a trade-off.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"The Second Conditional -- If I had... I would..." -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) for imagined situations and deals. It also shows the classic "if I would" mistake and how to fix it. Connection to Lesson 17: this is the structure you use to offer trade-offs and negotiate.

Tip: pause and build 5 sentences about a real customer request ("If we extended..., we would..."). Then say one realistic agreement out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — Difficult Conversations & Saying No Politely

Series
Ted Lasso -- Season 1: difficult conversations done with kindness

Watch how Ted handles tense moments and disappointing news without being cold or rude. Listen for the way characters soften bad news and stay warm under pressure. Connection to Lesson 18: saying the hard thing with tact, so the relationship survives the no.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 moments where someone delivers bad news politely, and note the softening words they use.

Watch ↗
Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant -- "How to say no" / difficult conversations

An organizational psychologist explores how to set boundaries, give honest answers, and handle pushback at work without burning the relationship. Listen for the words from this lesson (decline, boundary, honest, reassure, alternative) used in real workplace stories. Connection to Lesson 18: saying no with tact and offering a way forward.

Tip: pick one episode and note 5 polite phrases you could use to decline a customer request on your next call.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"How to Say No Politely in English" -- Business English phrases

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's language: softening a refusal with "I'm afraid", "Unfortunately" and "We're not able to", then offering an alternative. Watch for the polite intonation and how each no is followed by a way forward. Connection to Lesson 18: the phrases that let you decline a request and keep the customer.

Tip: pause after each example and say a polite no out loud for one of Mark's three requests, using the softener you just heard.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 19 — Renewals & Driving Value

Series
The Office (US) -- Season 3, Episode 2: "The Convention"

Watch how the sales team defends and renews client accounts by leading with results and a track record instead of price. Listen for how they demonstrate value to keep (retain) customers. Connection to Lesson 19: driving value and justifying a renewal.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 moments where someone shows a result ("we've delivered...", "we've grown..."). Note how each result is used to persuade.

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Gain Grow Retain Podcast -- "Driving Value at Renewal"

A Customer Success podcast about exactly your job: how to demonstrate value, justify a renewal with ROI, retain customers, and time an upsell. You hear the real phrases CS leaders use to turn results into renewals. Connection to Lesson 19: the language of your renewal calls.

Tip: listen twice. Note 5 present-perfect results ("we've grown...", "they've saved...") and 2 ways to justify a renewal with ROI.

Listen ↗
YouTube
"Present Perfect with FOR and SINCE" -- BBC Learning English

A clear, practical lesson on exactly today's grammar: the present perfect for results that are still true now, plus when to use "since" (a point in time) and "for" (a period). It shows the classic mistake of mixing them up. Connection to Lesson 19: this is the structure you use to demonstrate value and justify a renewal.

Tip: pause and build 5 sentences about a real result ("We've grown... since...", "The team has saved... for..."). Then say one line that justifies a renewal out loud.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 20 — Capstone — Quarterly Business Review (QBR)

Series
Succession -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Celebration"

Watch how senior executives present results, raise concerns and push for decisions in high-stakes business reviews. Listen for the present perfect ("we have grown"), comparatives ("better than last year") and the way people quote each other (reported speech). Connection to Lesson 20: a QBR is exactly this kind of meeting — results, concerns, and clear recommendations to stakeholders.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 sentences where someone reports results or quotes another person, and rewrite them as you would in a QBR.

Watch ↗
Podcast
The Gain Grow Retain Podcast -- "Running a Great QBR"

A Customer Success podcast with episodes on exactly your final lesson: how to structure a Quarterly Business Review, present highlights, handle a concern, and align stakeholders on next quarter. Listen for QBR vocabulary (summary, highlight, recommendation, objective, align) used by real CS leaders. Connection to Lesson 20: a model for the review you now know how to run in English.

Tip: pick one episode and write the QBR agenda you hear in five steps, using "first", "then", "after that" and "finally".

Listen ↗
YouTube
"How to Run a Customer Quarterly Business Review (QBR)" -- Customer Success training

A clear, practical walkthrough of a QBR from start to finish: the executive summary, the highlights, the metrics compared to last quarter, the recommendations and the plan for the next quarter. Watch how a presenter pairs each problem with a recommendation. Connection to Lesson 20: this is the structure you practised today, end to end.

Tip: pause after each section and say one sentence about a real account of yours, using the structure you just saw.

Watch on YouTube ↗
